title: Synapcity
subtitle: Validating a knowledge system for teams that lose decisions faster than they make them

sections.title: Why this problem matters
paragraphs: Teams rarely lose knowledge all at once. They lose it through thousands of small context leaks: decisions buried in chat, undocumented trade-offs, unresolved questions, and onboarding paths that depend on whoever happens to remember the history.
Synapcity started as an exploration of how to make team knowledge easier to recover without asking people to maintain a second system by hand.

pullQuote.text: The hard part is not storing knowledge. The hard part is making the right context reappear when a team needs it.

sections.title: The questions we had to answer

decisions.title: What counts as knowledge worth preserving?
decisions.teaser: A capture-everything system becomes noise almost immediately.
decisions.answer: The model focuses on decisions, source material, people, and follow-up questions rather than raw message archives. That keeps the product centered on reusable context instead of becoming another search box over stale conversation history.
alternatives: Index every message and rely on search ranking
Ask users to manually curate canonical documents
Treat every document, message, and task as the same object type

decisions.title: How should permissions work across team memory?
decisions.teaser: Knowledge tools fail if private context leaks into broad search results.
decisions.answer: The permission model is scoped around source systems and derived artifacts. If a user cannot access the original source, they should not see generated summaries or connected nodes that reveal its contents.
alternatives: Manual labels on every captured artifact
Separate indexes per source without cross-linking

decisions.title: When is AI useful versus distracting?
decisions.teaser: Generated summaries are only valuable when grounded in traceable source material.
decisions.answer: The product direction keeps AI in a supporting role: clustering related context, naming decision threads, and suggesting missing questions. Every generated artifact needs source links and a clear confidence boundary.

callout.text: The product is most useful when it treats knowledge as a graph of claims, decisions, people, and evidence, not a folder of documents.

designDecision.title: Source-Backed Knowledge Nodes
designDecision.description: Every durable piece of knowledge is represented as a node with links back to the source material that produced it.
designDecision.why: Teams need to trust the output. Source-backed nodes make generated context auditable instead of mysterious.

constraints.title: The source material is messy
constraints.description: Relevant context can live in Slack, docs, tickets, meeting notes, and people's heads. The system has to tolerate incomplete and uneven inputs.

constraints.title: Access control is product-critical
constraints.description: Search and summarization cannot reveal private source material through indirect references.

constraints.title: Users should not need a new habit
constraints.description: The product needs to recover context from existing workflows before asking users to create new artifacts.

sections.title: What is intentionally out of scope

tradeoffs.title: No full project management layer
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Focus on knowledge recovery and decision context
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Tasks and delivery planning still live in dedicated tools
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: Only if the knowledge graph starts exposing workflow gaps that existing tools cannot represent

tradeoffs.title: No automatic truth source
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Show source-backed context with confidence instead of declaring a single canonical answer
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Users still need judgment when sources disagree
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: When the system can model provenance, freshness, and authority well enough to rank truth claims responsibly

milestones.title: Problem framing
milestones.description: Mapped the recurring failure modes around buried decisions, onboarding context, and source fragmentation.

milestones.title: Knowledge model sketches
milestones.description: Explored how decisions, evidence, people, and unresolved questions should relate to each other.

milestones.title: Prototype scope
milestones.description: Define the smallest useful workflow that proves whether source-backed team memory is worth building.

reflections.insight: Knowledge products are trust products. If users cannot trace where an answer came from, they will treat the system as decoration.
reflections.transferable: Design AI-assisted systems around provenance before adding more generation.

reflections.insight: The capture problem is less important than the recovery problem. Teams already create huge amounts of context; the real value is making it usable later.
reflections.transferable: Optimize for the moment of retrieval, not the moment of storage.

sections.title: The work ahead
sections.intro: Synapcity is still in validation, so the next steps are intentionally narrow:

items.title: Prototype one source workflow
items.description: Start with one source system and prove whether extracted decisions can become useful knowledge nodes.

items.title: Test permission boundaries
items.description: Model what users can see when source permissions differ across people and teams.

items.title: Validate retrieval moments
items.description: Interview around concrete moments: onboarding, revisiting old decisions, and preparing for project handoff.

problem: Teams lose decisions, trade-offs, and source context across chat, docs, tickets, and meetings.
solution: A source-backed knowledge model that organizes decisions, evidence, people, and open questions instead of raw message archives.
role: Product discovery, knowledge model design, and prototype architecture.
proof: The prototype models knowledge as source-backed decisions, evidence, people, and open questions instead of raw message archives.
challenges: Separating durable knowledge from noisy source material
Preserving permission boundaries across generated summaries
Making context recoverable without asking teams to maintain a second system
learnings: Knowledge products are trust products; provenance has to come first.
The recovery moment matters more than the capture moment.
